edges.description: Shortened or absent matriglycan reduces alpha-dystroglycan size and laminin binding, destabilizing the muscle membrane during contraction.
edges.target: Sarcolemmal fragility and progressive muscle degeneration

edges.description: Severe dystroglycan glycosylation defects disrupt brain development, consistent with the type A cortical malformation branch.
intermediate_mechanisms: Loss of alpha-dystroglycan basement-membrane anchoring during cortical development.

edges.description: Severe dystroglycan glycosylation defects disrupt inhibitory synapse organization.
intermediate_mechanisms: Impaired CCK+/CB1R+ interneuron axon targeting and perisomatic inhibitory synapse assembly.
edges.target: Inhibitory synapse dysfunction and seizure susceptibility

edges.description: Severe alpha-DG glycosylation defects are associated with eye involvement in the dystroglycanopathy spectrum.
intermediate_mechanisms: Loss of alpha-dystroglycan laminin binding at ocular basement membranes.
edges.target: Retinal basement membrane disruption and eye malformations

edges.description: Defective alpha-DG glycosylation can perturb dystroglycan's broader adhesion-signaling role.
intermediate_mechanisms: Reduced ligand binding changes both mechanical adhesion and outside-inside dystroglycan signaling.

edges.description: Progressive muscle fiber degeneration manifests as proximally pronounced weakness.
edges.source: Sarcolemmal fragility and progressive muscle degeneration

edges.description: Recurrent sarcolemmal damage produces the dystrophic muscle pathology shared across the spectrum.
edges.source: Sarcolemmal fragility and progressive muscle degeneration

edges.description: Active muscle fiber injury and degeneration produce elevated circulating creatine kinase.
edges.source: Sarcolemmal fragility and progressive muscle degeneration

edges.description: Severe congenital muscle involvement can present at birth with profound hypotonia.
edges.source: Sarcolemmal fragility and progressive muscle degeneration

edges.description: Failed assembly and maintenance of the pial basement membrane creates glia limitans discontinuities.
Boundary breach mispositions marginal-zone cells before pyramidal neuron migration.
intermediate_mechanisms: Early pial basement membrane breach at the cortical surface.
edges.target: Cajal-Retzius Cell Mislocalization and Preplate Splitting Failure

edges.description: Pial basal lamina discontinuity destabilizes radial-glial basal anchoring.
Endfoot/scaffold detachment permits abnormal crossing of the pial boundary.
edges.target: Neuronal Overmigration Through Breached Glial Limitans

edges.description: Marginal-zone disorganization worsens abnormal cortical organization after pial boundary breach.
intermediate_mechanisms: Cajal-Retzius/subplate aggregation and preplate splitting failure.
edges.source: Cajal-Retzius Cell Mislocalization and Preplate Splitting Failure
edges.target: Neuronal Overmigration Through Breached Glial Limitans

edges.description: Neuronal overmigration through the pial boundary produces the cobblestone cortical malformation.
edges.source: Neuronal Overmigration Through Breached Glial Limitans

edges.description: The pathophysiologic cobblestone malformation is observed clinically as type II lissencephaly in severe type A disease.
Dystroglycanopathy brain involvement can include cognitive impairment and intellectual disability.
Severe type A disease can include progressive hydrocephalus.
intermediate_mechanisms: Severe WWS-pattern brainstem and cerebellar involvement.

edges.description: Inhibitory synaptic defects increase seizure susceptibility.
edges.source: Inhibitory synapse dysfunction and seizure susceptibility

edges.description: Severe ocular involvement in dystroglycanopathy can include retinal dysplasia.
intermediate_mechanisms: Severe dystroglycanopathy eye involvement through defective basement-membrane organization.
edges.source: Retinal basement membrane disruption and eye malformations

edges.description: Reduced alpha-dystroglycan glycosylation directly reports the primary matriglycan biosynthesis defect.
Reduced laminin binding reports loss of functional alpha-DG matriglycan.

nodes.description: In Pomgnt2-knockout dystroglycanopathy mice, pial basement membrane breaches coincide with ectopic clustering of Cajal-Retzius and subplate cells before pyramidal neurons start migrating. This perturbs preplate splitting and amplifies cortical disorganization.
nodes.id: Cajal-Retzius Cell Mislocalization and Preplate Splitting Failure

evidence.reference_title: Ectopic clustering of Cajal-Retzius and subplate cells is an initial pathological feature in Pomgnt2-knockout mice, a model of dystroglycanopathy.
evidence.snippet: Using the Pomgnt2-knockout (KO) mouse as a dystroglycanopathy model, we show that breaches of the pial basement membrane appeared at embryonic day 11.5, coinciding with the ectopic clustering of Cajal-Retzius cells and subplate neurons and prior to the migration onset of pyramidal neurons.

evidence.reference_title: Ectopic clustering of Cajal-Retzius and subplate cells is an initial pathological feature in Pomgnt2-knockout mice, a model of dystroglycanopathy.
evidence.snippet: Furthermore, in the Pomgnt2-KO cerebral cortex, preplate splitting failure likely occurred due to the aggregation of Cajal-Retzius and subplate cells, and migrating pyramidal neurons lost polarity and radial orientation.

nodes.description: The final type A dystroglycanopathy brain endpoint is cobblestone lissencephaly, produced by neuronal overmigration through a breached glial limitans. This is a pial-boundary overmigration disorder, not a primary failure of neuronal locomotion.

evidence.reference_title: Ectopic clustering of Cajal-Retzius and subplate cells is an initial pathological feature in Pomgnt2-knockout mice, a model of dystroglycanopathy.
evidence.snippet: Our findings demonstrate the initial pathological events in dystroglycanopathy mice and contribute to our understanding of how dystroglycan dysfunction affects brain development and progresses to cobblestone lissencephaly.

nodes.description: Type II (cobblestone) lissencephaly is the hallmark brain malformation of type A dystroglycanopathy, resulting from neuronal overmigration through breached glial limitans.

evidence.reference_title: Ectopic clustering of Cajal-Retzius and subplate cells is an initial pathological feature in Pomgnt2-knockout mice, a model of dystroglycanopathy.
evidence.snippet: Aberrant glycosylation of dystroglycan causes congenital muscular dystrophies associated with cobblestone lissencephaly, classified as dystroglycanopathy.

nodes.description: The core mechanism shared by all dystroglycanopathies. Alpha-dystroglycan requires extensive O-mannosyl glycosylation to generate matriglycan, a repeating disaccharide polymer that serves as the laminin-binding moiety. At least 18 genes encode enzymes in this sequential biosynthetic pathway: POMT1/POMT2 initiate O-mannosylation, POMGNT2/B3GALNT2/POMK elaborate the core M3 glycan, FKTN/FKRP/CRPPA add ribitol-phosphate linkers, and LARGE1 polymerizes the terminal matriglycan. Mutations in any of these genes reduce or abolish matriglycan, preventing alpha-DG from binding laminin, agrin, and other extracellular matrix proteins.

evidence.reference_title: Muscular Dystrophy with Ribitol-Phosphate Deficiency: A Novel Post-Translational Mechanism in Dystroglycanopathy.
evidence.snippet: Abnormal glycosylation of alpha-dystroglycan reduces its binding activities to ligand proteins, including laminins

evidence.reference_title: Muscular Dystrophy with Ribitol-Phosphate Deficiency: A Novel Post-Translational Mechanism in Dystroglycanopathy.
evidence.snippet: The normal sugar chain contains tandem structures of ribitol-phosphate, a pentose alcohol that was previously unknown in humans. The dystroglycanopathy genes fukutin, fukutin-related protein (FKRP), and isoprenoid synthase domain-containing protein (ISPD) encode essential enzymes for the synthesis of this structure
a shorter form of matriglycan reduce the size of α-DG and decrease laminin binding, leading to various forms of muscular dystrophy

evidence.reference_title: Dystroglycanopathy: From Elucidation of Molecular and Pathological Mechanisms to Development of Treatment Methods.
evidence.snippet: At least 18 causative genes of dystroglycanopathy have been identified, and its clinical symptoms are diverse, ranging from severe congenital to adult-onset limb-girdle types

nodes.description: Beyond its mechanical role, dystroglycan functions as a signaling hub. Ligand-bound alpha-DG influences beta-DG cytodomain phosphorylation and adaptor recruitment, modulating PI3K/AKT and ERK signaling pathways. This dual role implies that dystroglycanopathies involve both mechanical failure and signaling dysregulation, potentially contributing to impaired muscle regeneration and survival.

evidence.snippet: DG is a versatile molecule acting not only as a mechanical building block but also as a modulator of outside–inside signaling events

nodes.description: Markedly elevated serum CK levels reflect ongoing muscle fiber necrosis and are consistently found across all severity types.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical long-time course, novel mutations and genotype-phenotype correlation in a cohort of 27 families with POMT1-related disorders.
evidence.snippet: The milder LGMD phenotypes constantly show markedly elevated creatine kinase values in combination with microcephaly and cognitive impairment

nodes.description: Progressive hydrocephalus is common in type A, particularly in Walker-Warburg syndrome. May require ventriculoperitoneal shunting.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical long-time course, novel mutations and genotype-phenotype correlation in a cohort of 27 families with POMT1-related disorders.
evidence.snippet: a severe and progressive hydrocephalus with involvement of brainstem and/or cerebellum

nodes.description: Beyond structural brain malformations, alpha-DG glycosylation plays a critical role in organizing functional inhibitory synapses. In the hippocampus, dystroglycan is required for CCK+/CB1R+ basket interneuron axon targeting and perisomatic inhibitory synapse assembly. Severe hypoglycosylation disrupts these inhibitory circuits, reducing GABAergic transmission and increasing seizure susceptibility. This represents a distinct neurological mechanism from cobblestone lissencephaly.
nodes.id: Inhibitory synapse dysfunction and seizure susceptibility

evidence.snippet: these data show that inhibitory synaptic defects and elevated seizure susceptibility are hallmarks of severe dystroglycanopathy, and show that Dystroglycan plays an important role in organizing functional inhibitory synapse assembly

nodes.description: Profound intellectual disability is present in type A. Variable intellectual disability occurs in type B, ranging from mild to severe. Type C patients may have mild cognitive impairment or normal cognition.
Dystrophic changes on muscle biopsy with fiber size variation, necrosis, and fibrosis are present across all types.
Severe neonatal hypotonia is a presenting feature of type A and some type B dystroglycanopathies. In the most severe forms, infants are profoundly hypotonic at birth.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical long-time course, novel mutations and genotype-phenotype correlation in a cohort of 27 families with POMT1-related disorders.
evidence.snippet: patients with a severe WWS manifestation predominantly present with profound neonatal muscular hypotonia

nodes.description: Severe alpha-DG hypoglycosylation produces a breached pial boundary rather than a primary intrinsic migration arrest. Neurons and related cortical cells cross the disrupted glia limitans, creating ectopic tissue at the cortical surface.
nodes.id: Neuronal Overmigration Through Breached Glial Limitans

evidence.reference_title: GPR56 regulates pial basement membrane integrity and cortical lamination.
evidence.snippet: There are four crucial events in the development of cobblestone cortex, namely defective pial basement membrane (BM), abnormal anchorage of radial glial endfeet, mislocalized Cajal-Retzius cells, and neuronal overmigration.

evidence.reference_title: Ectopic clustering of Cajal-Retzius and subplate cells is an initial pathological feature in Pomgnt2-knockout mice, a model of dystroglycanopathy.
evidence.snippet: Furthermore, in the Pomgnt2-KO cerebral cortex, preplate splitting failure likely occurred due to the aggregation of Cajal-Retzius and subplate cells, and migrating pyramidal neurons lost polarity and radial orientation.

nodes.description: Loss of functional alpha-dystroglycan matriglycan and laminin binding weakens basement membrane assembly at the pial surface. In the severe type A brain branch, this disrupts the glia limitans before pyramidal neuron migration is complete.

evidence.reference_title: A role for dystroglycan in basement membrane assembly.
evidence.snippet: We find that dystroglycan is required for the formation of a basement membrane in embryoid bodies.

evidence.reference_title: A role for dystroglycan in basement membrane assembly.
evidence.snippet: Our results further indicate that dystroglycanlaminin interactions are prerequisite for the deposition of other basement membrane proteins.

nodes.description: Severe dystroglycan dysfunction causes discontinuities and breaches in the pial basal lamina/glia limitans. This boundary lesion is the proximal structural defect that precedes ectopic Cajal-Retzius/subplate clustering and neuronal overmigration in the cobblestone brain branch.

evidence.reference_title: Deletion of brain dystroglycan recapitulates aspects of congenital muscular dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: Dystroglycan-null brain loses its high-affinity binding to the extracellular matrix protein laminin, and shows discontinuities in the pial surface basal lamina (glia limitans) that probably underlie the neuronal migration errors.

evidence.reference_title: Ectopic clustering of Cajal-Retzius and subplate cells is an initial pathological feature in Pomgnt2-knockout mice, a model of dystroglycanopathy.
evidence.snippet: Using the Pomgnt2-knockout (KO) mouse as a dystroglycanopathy model, we show that breaches of the pial basement membrane appeared at embryonic day 11.5, coinciding with the ectopic clustering of Cajal-Retzius cells and subplate neurons and prior to the migration onset of pyramidal neurons.

nodes.description: Progressive proximal muscular weakness is the universal feature across all dystroglycanopathy types. In type A it presents as severe neonatal hypotonia, while in type C it may not manifest until childhood or adulthood.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical long-time course, novel mutations and genotype-phenotype correlation in a cohort of 27 families with POMT1-related disorders.
evidence.snippet: POMT1-related disorders belong to the group of dystroglycanopathies characterized by a proximally pronounced muscular dystrophy with structural or functional involvement of the brain and/or the eyes

nodes.description: Pial basement membrane disruption weakens the anchoring of radial glial basal endfeet at the glia limitans. Loss of this scaffold removes a boundary and polarity cue needed to constrain cortical organization during the type A dystroglycanopathy brain branch.

evidence.reference_title: GPR56 regulates pial basement membrane integrity and cortical lamination.
evidence.snippet: There are four crucial events in the development of cobblestone cortex, namely defective pial basement membrane (BM), abnormal anchorage of radial glial endfeet, mislocalized Cajal-Retzius cells, and neuronal overmigration.

evidence.reference_title: Ectopic clustering of Cajal-Retzius and subplate cells is an initial pathological feature in Pomgnt2-knockout mice, a model of dystroglycanopathy.
evidence.snippet: Furthermore, in the Pomgnt2-KO cerebral cortex, preplate splitting failure likely occurred due to the aggregation of Cajal-Retzius and subplate cells, and migrating pyramidal neurons lost polarity and radial orientation.
-mannose kinase (POMK) is required for LARGE1 to generate full-length matriglycan on α-DG (~150–250 kDa)

readouts.interpretation: Reduced alpha-dystroglycan glycosylation directly reports the primary matriglycan biosynthesis defect.

evidence.reference_title: Muscular Dystrophy with Ribitol-Phosphate Deficiency: A Novel Post-Translational Mechanism in Dystroglycanopathy.
evidence.snippet: Abnormal glycosylation of alpha-dystroglycan reduces its binding activities to ligand proteins, including laminins

readouts.interpretation: Reduced laminin binding reports loss of functional alpha-DG matriglycan.

nodes.description: Alpha-DG in the retinal inner limiting membrane binds laminin to maintain retinal architecture during development. Severe alpha-DG hypoglycosylation disrupts this basement membrane, causing retinal dysplasia, anterior chamber malformations, and in the most severe cases microphthalmos. Like brain involvement, eye pathology requires near-complete loss of glycosylation and is predominantly seen in type A.
nodes.id: Retinal basement membrane disruption and eye malformations

nodes.description: Structural retinal abnormalities occur primarily in type A dystroglycanopathy as a consequence of retinal inner limiting membrane disruption during development.

evidence.reference_title: Ocular findings in lissencephaly.
evidence.snippet: Ocular abnormalities included optic nerve hypoplasia and atrophy, retinal dysplasia, retinal nonattachment, macular hypoplasia, anterior segment malformation, and strabismus.

nodes.description: Loss of alpha-DG-laminin binding disrupts the dystrophin-glycoprotein complex (DGC) link between the intracellular cytoskeleton and the extracellular basement membrane. This renders the sarcolemma mechanically fragile, leading to contraction-induced damage, repeated cycles of muscle fiber necrosis and regeneration, progressive fibrosis, and fatty replacement. This mechanism is common to all severity types and underlies the progressive proximal muscular weakness and elevated serum creatine kinase characteristic of the disease.
nodes.id: Sarcolemmal fragility and progressive muscle degeneration

evidence.snippet: This smaller form of α-DG binds laminin and maintains specific force but does not prevent muscle pathophysiology, including reduced force production after eccentric contractions (ECs) or abnormalities in the neuromuscular junctions
represents a molecular bridge between the outside and the inside of the cell, which is essential for the mechanical and structural stability of the plasma membrane

nodes.description: Epileptic seizures occur in type A and some type B patients. In addition to structural cortical malformation, seizure susceptibility results from impaired inhibitory synapse assembly involving CCK+/CB1R+ basket interneurons.

evidence.snippet: these data show that inhibitory synaptic defects and elevated seizure susceptibility are hallmarks of severe dystroglycanopathy
